museum.name: Gaming Time Machine
museum.dek: A short winter drawer: Dig Dug appears in Japan, Ms. Pac-Man spreads through arcades, and British microcomputer anticipation gathers around magazines and adverts.
museum.curatorNote: February relies on a few solid anchors and a lot of carefully marked context. The safest release anchor is Dig Dug's Japanese arcade date.

sections.title: News
sections.subtitle: The arcade boom keeps producing new shapes.

items.title: Dig Dug releases in Japan
items.body: Namco's underground maze-action game arrived in Japan, giving 1982 another characterful arcade system of tunnels, pumps and timing.
items.sourceNote: Dig Dug references list Japan as February 20, 1982.

items.title: Ms. Pac-Man moves from record to reality
items.body: Where January holds the copyright date, February is a safe way to describe the cabinet's broader early-1982 arcade presence.
items.sourceNote: Pac-Man Wiki notes late-January/early-February release ambiguity.

items.title: Atari 2600 Pac-Man becomes imminent
items.body: The home version of Pac-Man was close enough to dominate expectations, even before disappointment had a shape.
items.sourceNote: Atari 2600 Pac-Man appears in March with April 3 promotional Pac-Man Day.

items.title: UK readers watch the microcomputer price war
items.body: The Spectrum was coming, the BBC Micro had prestige, and cheaper machines made games feel domestic.
items.sourceNote: UK microcomputer context around ZX81, BBC Micro, Spectrum and VIC-20.

items.title: Arcade characters keep replacing abstraction
items.body: Ms. Pac-Man, Zaxxon, Dig Dug and Donkey Kong pointed toward games that players remembered by character and cabinet, not just score.
items.sourceNote: Based on early-1982 arcade release context.

sections.title: Releases
sections.subtitle: Eight February shelf and release-window objects, with context marked clearly.

items.title: Dig Dug
items.body: Japanese release of Namco's underground arcade hit.
items.sourceNote: Dig Dug references list February 20, 1982 in Japan.

items.title: Ms. Pac-Man
items.body: Early-1982 rollout context for Midway's sequel.

items.title: Zaxxon
items.body: Still fresh from January, spreading the idea that arcade screens could fake depth.

items.title: Pac-Man
items.body: Not out yet, but anticipation was release culture in itself.

items.title: Donkey Kong
items.body: Still a major cabinet presence, reinforcing character-led design.

items.title: BBC Micro software
items.body: Early BBC Micro owners were beginning to treat the machine as a games platform as well as an educational one.

items.title: ZX81 cassette games
items.body: The British low-cost computer scene already had a cassette-software habit before the Spectrum arrived.

items.title: VIC-20 games
items.body: Commodore's existing colour home computer kept the idea of affordable home games alive while the C64 waited.
items.sourceNote: VIC-20 context; not a February release claim.

sections.title: Hardware
sections.subtitle: A month of machines becoming categories.

items.title: Arcade cabinet variety
items.body: Maze, shooter and character games were branching into distinctive silhouettes.

items.title: Atari VCS expectation
items.body: The VCS/2600 still looked like the obvious home answer to the arcade, just before Pac-Man tested that promise.

items.title: BBC Micro authority
items.body: The BBC Micro's education aura mattered in Britain: it made home computing feel sanctioned, even when used for games.

items.title: Cassette loading
items.body: A tape was software, storage and ritual all at once.

sections.title: Magazine Covers
sections.subtitle: The shelf talked before the machines did.

items.title: C&VG February-style coverage
items.headline: Arcades meet home micros
items.coverImageAlt: Reconstructed C&VG style cover for February 1982.
items.curatorNote: C&VG's value was the mix: coin-op games beside machines a reader might own.
items.body: C&VG's value was the mix: coin-op games beside machines a reader might own.

items.title: Electronic Games arcade reporting
items.headline: The American boom in print
items.coverImageAlt: Reconstructed Electronic Games style cover for February 1982.
items.curatorNote: The US magazine voice made arcade culture feel organized and named.
items.body: The US magazine voice made arcade culture feel organized and named.

items.title: Personal Computer World
items.headline: Home computers before home games fully settle
items.coverImageAlt: Reconstructed PCW style cover for February 1982.
items.curatorNote: A serious magazine for machines that players would use unseriously at night.
items.body: A serious magazine for machines that players would use unseriously at night.

items.title: Popular Computing Weekly
items.headline: Weekly adverts and listings
items.coverImageAlt: Reconstructed Popular Computing Weekly style cover for February 1982.
items.curatorNote: The weekly format made the micro scene feel alive and local.
items.body: The weekly format made the micro scene feel alive and local.

sections.title: Online Life
sections.subtitle: Discovery travelled by paper and proximity.

items.title: Watching was learning
items.body: Arcade technique travelled by standing near a cabinet.

items.title: Listings were code distribution
items.body: A printed program was a game waiting for a careful typist.

items.title: Adverts were storefronts
items.body: Mail-order boxes in magazines worked like slow hyperlinks.

items.title: The modem was not ordinary
items.body: Networking existed, but not as everyday gaming culture.

sections.title: What It Felt Like
sections.subtitle: February had the mood of an arcade aisle warming up.

items.title: New cabinets felt sudden
items.body: A game could simply appear one week and rearrange the room.

items.title: The home version was a promise
items.body: Players wanted the arcade at home before they knew how compromised it could be.

items.title: Computers felt expandable
items.body: A keyboard implied more than the game in front of you.

items.title: The year was still optimistic
items.body: Nothing yet had taught the shelf to be cautious.

sources.label: Dig Dug release context
sources.note: Used for February 20 Japan and April international context.

editorNotes: Initial AI-generated research draft; needs human editorial review and source verification.
